A toy benchmark inspired by Neural Computers

Can a Model Learn How a Computer Works?

This notebook studies a simple version of that question: given the current terminal screen, can a model predict the next one purely from observed screen transitions?

Source paper: Neural Computers (2026)

Question
Can next-screen prediction recover both typing mechanics and command meaning?
Benchmark
A toy terminal task with matched saved results for MLP, Transformer, and GRU baselines.
Result
MLP wins overall; Transformer is more competitive on the meaning-heavy Enter step.

data.text/markdown:
What exactly was measured?

Before looking at the winner, here is the benchmark in plain English.

data.text/html:
Task
One sample is a terminal episode step: current screen, action, and next screen.
Main metric
Changed-cell accuracy scores only the cells that actually changed.
Evaluation settings
The saved benchmark covers standard and paraphrase settings, with family-level and exact-command hints.
Comparison rule
All models are compared on the same toy task with the same saved evaluation settings.

<marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\"><strong>Why changed-cell accuracy?</strong> Most of the screen never changes. A model can look impressive on ordinary character accuracy while still failing the actual prediction problem.</span></span>"' data-kind='"warn"'></marimo-callout-output>

data.text/markdown:
Watch the toy computer

Before the benchmark numbers, here is the task itself as a tiny interactive terminal. Pick a command and scrub through time.

data.text/html: <marimo-ui-element object-id='RGSE-0' random-id='0d5ab137-bc4c-d3e4-1f62-cb3ca9ced168'><marimo-dropdown data-initial-value='["phrasing 1"]' data-label='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\">Command phrasing</span></span>"' data-options='["phrasing 1","phrasing 2","phrasing 3"]' data-allow-select-none='false' data-searchable='false' data-full-width='false'></marimo-dropdown></marimo-ui-element>

<marimo-ui-element object-id='emfo-0' random-id='221ba7e0-914e-02a9-dd01-bb4b45a8fca3'><marimo-slider data-initial-value='0' data-label='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\">Step through time</span></span>"' data-start='0' data-stop='8' data-step='1' data-steps='[]' data-debounce='false' data-disabled='false' data-orientation='"horizontal"' data-show-value='false' data-include-input='false' data-full-width='true'></marimo-slider></marimo-ui-element>

Command: whoami
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        
                                        
research@nc:~$ █                        
Start state Step 0/8 0 changed cells

<marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\"><strong>Notice the asymmetry:</strong> typing usually changes one tiny patch, but pressing Enter can change many cells at once.\nThat is why the benchmark separates easy mechanics from harder command meaning.</span></span>"' data-kind='"info"'></marimo-callout-output>

data.text/markdown:
The core challenge: easy vs. hard

The toy computer asks models to learn two very different skills.

data.text/html:
Local update

Mechanics

“The user typed w, so show w.”

Easier
Semantic update

Meaning

“The user ran whoami, so show the username.”

Harder

<marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\"><strong>The scientific question:</strong> Does a model first learn how a computer <em>looks</em>, or how it actually <em>works</em>?</span>\n<span class=\"paragraph\">This benchmark suggests: surface mechanics come first. Command meaning is the harder part.</span></span>"' data-kind='"info"'></marimo-callout-output>

data.text/markdown:
How you would implement the core idea yourself

At a high level, this toy benchmark turns the paper’s idea into a simple loop: current screen in → next screen out.

data.text/html:
Input
1. Current screen
What the terminal looks like now.
→
Context
2. Optional hint
Command family or exact command.
→
Prediction
3. Model
Predict the full next screen.
→
Evaluation
4. Score
Measure accuracy on cells that changed.

An episode is a sequence of terminal frames. Each step contains:
  • the current screen
  • the action that happened (type_char, enter, idle, ...)
  • the next screen
That makes the supervised learning target very concrete: predict the next frame.
The simplest baseline is local:
  • look at the current screen
  • maybe include a hint about the command
  • predict the next screen
Then compare that local baseline against sequence models like a Transformer or GRU.
Do not rely only on plain character accuracy. Most of the screen usually stays the same, so a model can look good while missing the important part. The better metric here is:
  • changed-cell accuracy = accuracy restricted to cells that actually changed
for episode in episodes:
for t in range(len(frames) - 1):
    x = current_screen[t], optional_hint[t]
    y = next_screen[t + 1]
    loss = screen_prediction_loss(model(x), y)
Then evaluate on changed-cell accuracy, typing-only steps, and Enter-only steps.

<marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\"><strong>Loaded benchmark files</strong> from <code>/mnt/d/alpha-proj/experiments/toy_nc_cli/results</code>. The charts below use saved experiment results, not hand-entered demo values.</span></span>"' data-kind='"success"'></marimo-callout-output>

data.text/markdown:
The architecture showdown

Now for the main comparison: same toy task, same four evaluation settings, three different model families.

data.text/html:
Main comparison outcome

Mean overall changed-cell accuracy
74.5%
MLP is best overall in 4/4 settings.
Mean Enter-step accuracy
57.2%
Transformer beats MLP on Enter in 3/4 settings.
Mean overall changed-cell accuracy
19.9%
GRU was a negative result on this benchmark.
The honest story: the MLP is still the strongest toy baseline overall, but the Transformer is more competitive on the meaning-heavy Enter step.
That advantage is real, but it is not universal: it is strongest in the standard setting and much smaller under paraphrase.

<marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\"><strong>Read this chart carefully:</strong> positive bars mean the Transformer is better than the MLP on Enter. The gain is huge in the easiest standard-family setting, modest in standard-command, and nearly disappears under paraphrase.</span></span>"' data-kind='"info"'></marimo-callout-output>

data.text/markdown:
Model profile

Below is a more honest summary than a single winner badge: mean performance across the four settings.

data.text/html: <marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\"><strong>Metric trap:</strong> all three models look similar on plain character accuracy — MLP 97.2%, Transformer 97.3%, GRU 96.8%.</span>\n<span class=\"paragraph\">But changed-cell accuracy tells the real story, because it ignores the easy unchanged background.</span></span>"' data-kind='"warn"'></marimo-callout-output>

data.text/markdown:
Mechanics vs. meaning

This view compresses the whole story into one picture:
  • farther right = better at typing/mechanics
  • higher up = better at Enter/meaning
  • larger bubble = better overall changed-cell accuracy

data.text/html: <marimo-callout-output data-html='"<span class=\"markdown prose dark:prose-invert contents\"><span class=\"paragraph\"><strong>Why this chart matters:</strong> the MLP sits farther right because it is strongest at local mechanics. The Transformer sits higher because it is relatively stronger on Enter. The GRU stays in the lower-left corner.</span></span>"' data-kind='"success"'></marimo-callout-output>

Best cell in each metric block is highlighted. Numbers come directly from the saved JSON/CSV results.
Setting Overall changed-cell accuracy Enter-step changed-cell accuracy
MLP Transformer GRU MLP Transformer GRU
Standard / family hint 68.1% 64.4% 19.4% 48.4% 81.6% 12.1%
Standard / exact command 94.7% 47.6% 21.8% 48.6% 57.8% 13.4%
Paraphrase / family hint 63.7% 43.4% 18.8% 51.8% 47.2% 17.0%
Paraphrase / exact command 71.6% 49.6% 19.5% 41.3% 42.1% 17.2%

data.text/markdown:
Takeaway

Here is the clearest evidence-backed story this toy benchmark supports.

data.text/html:
01
Best overall model
The MLP is still the strongest baseline on the main benchmark metric.
02
Most interesting contrast
The Transformer is relatively stronger on meaning-heavy Enter steps.
03
Important limit
That Transformer gain shrinks sharply under paraphrase, so the toy task still does not show deep semantic generalization.

• MLP is a strong baseline when the task is mostly local and mechanical.
  • Transformer becomes interesting when the task depends more on global context or command semantics.
  • GRU did not help here, so “add recurrence” is not enough by itself.
The full Neural Computers paper works at a much larger scale: real computer recordings, video generation models, and much more data. This toy notebook does not replicate that paper. Instead, it builds intuition for a smaller question: what can a model learn from screen transitions alone, and where does that break?
  1. Use a stronger semantic task than fixed toy commands.
  2. Add true held-out semantic generalization, not just phrasing variants.
  3. Test a hybrid story: an MLP-style local mechanism plus a Transformer-style global context path.

data.text/markdown:
Built with marimo

Inspired by Neural Computers (Zhuge et al., 2026)

This competition notebook uses saved local experiment artifacts for reproducible presentation.
